slides.headline: Simulating Open Democracy
slides.subhead: Comparing deliberation engines in a shared argument-theoretic environment.
slides.notes: Open with the central comparative question: does cognitive implementation change the kind of deliberation an ABM produces, once the forum, argument pool, and outcome metrics are held fixed? The talk reports one answer from the current manuscript.

items.title: Ignacio Urbina
items.detail: Department of Political Science, Stony Brook University

items.title: Design
items.detail: Single-group town hall · empirical rule-based and tool-constrained LLM engines

slides.headline: Cognition is an unspecified degree of freedom in deliberation ABMs.
slides.subhead: Empirical forums bundle design levers; classical opinion-dynamics models bake cognition into the update rule.
slides.notes: Empirical deliberation research (Barabas 2004; Fishkin et al. 2021; Niemeyer et al. 2024) cannot cleanly isolate individual levers because real forums vary along several dimensions at once. Opinion-dynamics ABMs (DeGroot 1974; Deffuant et al. 2000; Hegselmann & Krause 2002) offer controlled comparison but usually fix the cognitive representation inside a single update equation. The research question follows directly from that gap.

items.title: Empirical forums
items.detail: facilitation, composition, duration, and closure are co-varied (Niemeyer et al. 2024)

items.title: Opinion-dynamics ABMs
items.detail: cognition enters as the update rule (DeGroot 1974; Hegselmann & Krause 2002)

items.title: Blind spot
items.detail: a design recommendation rests on an unstated cognitive theory of citizens

right.label: Research question
right.text: Does cognitive implementation change the kind of deliberation an ABM produces, once the forum, argument pool, and outcome metrics are held fixed?

slides.headline: Reason-level ontology, swappable cognitive engines.
slides.subhead: Agents hold weighted repertoires over a shared pool; opinions are derived from those reasons.
slides.notes: State space is (considerations C, attack-support graph A, sparse repertoires R_i with signed weights w_{ic}). Expressed opinion o_i = clip(mean over R_i of w_{ic} · d_c). Deliberation unfolds through Voice → Evaluate → Reflect, with one voice opportunity per agent per round. The three hooks are the abstraction boundary of the model: any engine implementing them can be plugged in.

stages.title: Voice
stages.detail: one consideration per agent, per round

stages.title: Evaluate
stages.detail: score influence of what was heard

slides.headline: Seattle 2014 $15/hour Polis conversation.
slides.notes: Not a binding mini-public: CompDemocracy documents this case as a long-running demonstration conversation, tied to Seattle's real 2014 fight over the $15 minimum wage — phase-in schedule, employer coverage, tips and benefits, enforcement. This grounds the scenario in a live policy controversy rather than an abstract prompt.

items.label: Statements
items.detail: 30 moderated statements retained for scenario construction

blocks.label: Policy domain
blocks.text: Minimum-wage phase-in, employer coverage, tips, and enforcement — a documented controversy with publicly available reasons on both sides.

slides.headline: One vote matrix grounds the pool, the relation graph, and agent initialization.
slides.subhead: Sparse initialization: agents begin only with the considerations they actually voted on (mean ≈ 5.4 of 10).
slides.notes: Three distinct uses of the same Polis export: (1) moderated statements supply the argument pool; (2) participant co-voting identifies attack/support structure and persuasiveness via Yule's Q; (3) an Ising-based reconstruction of belief profiles seeds agent initialization. New considerations enter later through the Reflect hook once the engine clears an adoption threshold.

cells: shared reasons available to voice and adopt

slides.headline: Two engines, three compositions, eight rounds.
slides.subhead: The forum is held fixed; only the cognitive operator varies between arms.
slides.notes: Identical town-hall protocol, argument pool, round count, and outcome metrics across arms. The rule-based engine is empirically grounded through the co-vote structure; the LLM engine is tool-constrained (no gold demonstrations, no task-specific fine-tuning). Arm sample sizes are uneven by design stage — 600 rule-based vs. 286 LLM — so the comparison is reported as directional slope contrasts rather than point-estimate decompositions.

slides.headline: The LLM engine tightens the link between opinions and reasons.
slides.subhead: Positive LLM − rule-based slope gaps on DRI and meta-consensus (triplets: polarized / symmetric / three-cluster).
slides.notes: DRI asks whether opinion similarity is grounded in underlying reason structure (Niemeyer et al. 2024; Veri & Niemeyer 2025). Meta-consensus tracks convergence on what matters rather than on what to do. The LLM signature is largest on three-cluster rooms for DRI and uniformly positive and significant on meta-consensus. Two supporting outcomes are not shown on-slide: plurality moves downward in both arms, and better-argument-wins also favors the LLM engine in every room but is not part of the core epistemic-alignment claim.

items.label: DRI slope gap (LLM − rule)
items.detail: three-cluster largest (+9.21, p = .012); polarized clear (+5.72, p = .001); symmetric imprecise (p = .422)

items.label: Meta-consensus slope gap
items.detail: positive and statistically significant in all three compositions

blocks.label: Interpretation
blocks.text: The LLM engine more often reorganizes what agents take to matter, not only what they say; reason-level alignment improves even where opinion-level convergence does not.

slides.headline: The rule-based engine keeps reasons visible and moving.
slides.subhead: Negative LLM − rule-based slope gaps on argument diversity across all rooms, and on cross-camp uptake in the symmetric room.
slides.notes: Argument diversity rises under both engines, but much more under the rule-based arm; the gap is roughly -21 index points in every composition (all p < .001). Cross-camp uptake leans rule-based in symmetric (-3.64, p = .043) and three-cluster rooms, is near zero in polarized. Plurality (not shown) also favors rule-based. The overall pattern is argumentative activation and circulation without the same reason-level consolidation the LLM arm produces.

items.label: Argument diversity slope gap
items.detail: rule-based advantage in every composition; all p < .001

items.label: Cross-camp uptake slope gap
items.detail: clearest rule-based edge in the symmetric room (p = .043); null elsewhere

blocks.label: Interpretation
blocks.text: The rule-based engine preserves argumentative activation: more reasons circulate across camps, even when that circulation does not consolidate into shared reason structure.

slides.headline: Different cognitive regimes under identical forums.
slides.subhead: Epistemic integration and argumentative activation can come apart.
slides.notes: Close with the manuscript's bounded reading. This is a directional comparative exercise with uneven arm sizes, one issue domain, and six-agent rooms — not a causal decomposition of every metric. The two engines instantiate different models of successful deliberation rather than different parameterizations of one mechanism. The contribution is framework-level: a way to compare cognitive implementations under a shared forum. Tie back to Landemore's (2020) open-democracy agenda — if randomly selected bodies are to do more than register preferences, they need ways to inspect whether visible agreement is grounded in shared justificatory structure.

items: LLM engine → stronger reason-level alignment (DRI, meta-consensus).
Rule-based engine → stronger argumentative circulation (diversity, uptake in symmetric rooms).
Composition moderates magnitude; the claim is directional, not uniform.

blocks.label: Scope & implication
blocks.text: Bounded comparative exercise — one issue, six-agent rooms, 600 vs. 286 runs; not a substitute for citizens. Engine choice should be reported as part of the design result, not held fixed as a technical detail.
